ped.response:
**PED (Pre-Existing Disease)** refers to any medical condition or illness that was diagnosed, had symptoms, or was treated before the insurance policy was purchased.

**Key IRDAI Rules on PED:**
• **Waiting Period**: PED exclusions cannot exceed **48 months** (4 years) of continuous coverage under IRDAI Health Insurance Regulations, 2016
• **After 4 years**: The insurer **must** cover pre-existing conditions — they cannot deny claims on PED grounds
• **Portability**: When you port your policy, the waiting period credit from the old policy carries over (IRDAI Portability Guidelines)
• **Disclosure**: You must declare pre-existing conditions at policy purchase. Non-disclosure can lead to claim rejection

**Common PED examples**: Diabetes, Hypertension, Thyroid disorders, Asthma, Heart conditions

**If your claim was denied due to PED** and you've completed the 48-month waiting period, the denial is **incorrect** and you have strong grounds for appeal.

💡 *This is general information. For specific case advice, provide your policy details.*

irdai.response:
**IRDAI (Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India)** is the regulatory body for insurance in India.

**Key IRDAI Regulations for Health Insurance:**

📋 **Claim Settlement**
• Claims must be settled or rejected within **30 days** of receiving all documents
• If additional info is needed, insurer must communicate within **15 days**
• Cashless claims: Authorization within **1 hour** for planned, **2 hours** for emergency

📋 **PED Exclusion (IRDAI Health Insurance Regulations, 2016)**
• Maximum waiting period for PED: **48 months** of continuous coverage
• After this period, PED must be covered

📋 **Denial Requirements (IRDAI TPA Guidelines, 2016)**
• Insurer must cite **specific policy clause numbers** when denying
• Must provide **written, understandable reasons**
• Generic or vague denials violate IRDAI guidelines

📋 **Grievance Redressal**
• Every insurer must have a Grievance Redressal Officer
• Complaint must be acknowledged within **3 working days**
• Resolution within **15 working days**
• If unresolved, escalate to Insurance Ombudsman or IRDAI IGMS

📋 **Other Important Rules**
• No claim can be rejected for non-disclosure after **8 years** (moratorium period)
• Policy portability: Waiting period credits must be honored
• Renewability: Health policies must be renewed lifelong (IRDAI Master Circular, 2020)

🔗 *Visit [irdai.gov.in](https://irdai.gov.in) for official circulars*

denial.response:
**What to Do When Your Insurance Claim is Denied:**

**Step 1: Understand the Denial** 📝
• Read the denial letter carefully
• Note the specific reason and policy clause cited
• If no specific clause is cited, this itself violates IRDAI guidelines

**Step 2: Verify the Denial** 🔍
• Compare the denial reason against your actual policy terms
• Check if the cited exclusion actually applies to your case
• If it's a PED denial, verify if you've completed the 48-month waiting period

**Step 3: Gather Evidence** 📂
• Policy document (with relevant clauses highlighted)
• Medical records and discharge summary
• Treating doctor's recommendation/certificate
• Original bills and receipts

**Step 4: File an Appeal** ✉️
• Write to the Grievance Redressal Officer within 30 days
• Cite specific policy clauses and IRDAI regulations
• Include all supporting documents

**Step 5: Escalate if Needed** ⚖️
• Insurance Ombudsman (free, for claims up to ₹30 lakhs)
• Consumer Forum (for larger claims)
• IRDAI IGMS portal (igms.irda.gov.in)

**Common Denial Reasons:**
• Pre-existing condition exclusion
• Waiting period not completed
• Medical necessity not established
• Non-network hospital
• Insufficient documentation
• Policy lapsed or not in force

💡 *Use our Agent Pipeline to analyze your specific denial and generate an appeal letter.*

appeal.response:
**How to Appeal a Health Insurance Claim Denial:**

**Level 1 — Internal Appeal (with the insurer)** 📝
Write a formal appeal letter to the Grievance Redressal Officer:
• Cite specific policy clauses that support your claim
• Reference relevant IRDAI regulations
• Attach medical evidence of necessity
• Timeline: File within 30 days of denial
• Insurer must respond within 15 working days

**Level 2 — Insurance Ombudsman** ⚖️
If the insurer doesn't resolve within 30 days:
• **Free of cost** — no lawyer needed
• For claims up to **₹30 lakhs**
• Decision is **binding on the insurer** (not on you)
• File online at [ecoi.co.in](http://ecoi.co.in)
• Must file within 1 year of rejection
• There are 17 Ombudsman offices across India

**Level 3 — Consumer Forum / Court** 🏛️
• **District Forum**: Claims up to ₹1 crore (filing fee: ₹200-₹5,000)
• **State Commission**: ₹1 crore to ₹10 crore
• **National Commission**: Above ₹10 crore
• Can claim compensation for mental agony and legal costs

**Bonus — IRDAI IGMS Portal** 🌐
• File complaint at [igms.irda.gov.in](https://igms.irda.gov.in)
• IRDAI tracks and monitors all complaints
• Puts regulatory pressure on insurers

💡 *Our 'Generate Appeal' feature creates a professional appeal letter with IRDAI citations automatically!*

terms.response:
**Common Health Insurance Terms Explained:**

💰 **Sum Insured**: Maximum amount the insurer will pay during a policy period

💳 **Deductible**: Fixed amount you must pay before insurance kicks in

📊 **Co-pay/Copayment**: Percentage of the claim you pay yourself (common: 10-20%)

📏 **Sub-limit**: Maximum cap on specific expenses (e.g., room rent capped at 1% of sum insured per day)

⏳ **Waiting Period**: Time before certain conditions are covered:
  • Initial waiting period: 30 days (except accidents)
  • Specific disease: 2 years
  • Pre-existing disease: Up to 4 years (48 months)

🏥 **Cashless Claim**: Treatment at a network hospital where the insurer pays the hospital directly

💵 **Reimbursement Claim**: You pay first, then submit bills to get money back

🤝 **TPA (Third Party Administrator)**: Company that processes claims on behalf of the insurer

🔄 **Portability**: Switching your policy to another insurer while retaining waiting period credits

📈 **No Claim Bonus (NCB)**: Discount or increased sum insured for every claim-free year (typically 5-50%)

🏠 **Domiciliary Hospitalization**: Treatment at home when hospital beds are unavailable

🔧 **Daycare Procedures**: Treatments that don't require 24-hour hospitalization (e.g., cataract surgery, dialysis)

Ask me about any specific term and I'll explain it in detail!

claim_process.response:
**How to File a Health Insurance Claim:**

**Option A: Cashless Claim** (at network hospitals)
1. Show your health card at hospital admission
2. Hospital sends pre-authorization to insurer/TPA
3. Insurer approves within 1 hour (planned) or 2 hours (emergency)
4. Get treated — hospital bills insurer directly
5. You may need to pay deductible/co-pay amounts

**Option B: Reimbursement Claim** (at any hospital)
1. Get treated and pay the hospital
2. Collect all original bills, prescriptions, and discharge summary
3. Inform insurer within the policy's specified timeframe (usually 24-48 hours for emergency, 7 days for planned)
4. Submit claim form with all documents
5. Insurer processes within 30 days

**Documents Needed:**
📋 Duly filled claim form
📋 Policy copy/health card
📋 Hospital discharge summary
📋 All medical bills (itemized)
📋 Prescription and medicines bills
📋 Diagnostic test reports
📋 Doctor's consultation notes
📋 Pre-authorization form (for cashless)
📋 FIR copy (for accident cases)

**Important Timelines (IRDAI):**
• Inform insurer: Within 24-48 hours (emergency)
• Submit documents: Within 7-15 days of discharge
• Insurer decision: Within 30 days of receiving all documents

waiting_period.response:
**Health Insurance Waiting Periods Explained:**

⏳ **Initial Waiting Period**: **30 days** from policy start date
• No claims allowed (except accidents)
• Applies only to new policies (not renewals)

⏳ **Specific Disease Waiting Period**: **2 years** (24 months)
• Applies to listed illnesses like cataract, hernia, sinusitis, kidney stones, etc.
• The specific list varies by insurer — check your policy schedule

⏳ **Pre-Existing Disease (PED) Waiting Period**: Up to **4 years** (48 months)
• Maximum allowed by IRDAI
• After 48 months, PED **must** be covered
• Some policies offer shorter PED waiting (e.g., 2-3 years) — check your policy

⏳ **Maternity Waiting Period**: Usually **2-4 years**
• Varies by insurer

**Portability & Waiting Periods:**
• When you port to a new insurer, time already waited is credited
• Example: If you've had Policy A for 3 years, only 1 more year of PED waiting in Policy B
• IRDAI mandates portability credits must be honored

💡 *If your claim was denied for 'waiting period not completed,' verify the exact dates. Sometimes insurers miscalculate.*

medical_necessity.response:
**Medical Necessity in Insurance Claims:**

Medical necessity means the treatment was:
✅ Required to diagnose or treat the patient's condition
✅ Appropriate as per standard medical practice
✅ Following evidence-based clinical guidelines
✅ Not being performed for convenience or comfort

**If your claim was denied for 'not medically necessary':**

1. **Get a certificate from your treating doctor** stating why the treatment was necessary
2. **Reference clinical guidelines** — ACC/AHA, WHO, or specialty guidelines that support the treatment
3. **Cite the diagnosis** — provide ICD-10 codes and corresponding standard treatments
4. **IRDAI stance**: Insurers **cannot** overrule a qualified doctor's recommendation without getting an independent medical opinion

**Important IRDAI Rule:**
If the insurer disputes medical necessity, they must:
• Get an independent medical opinion from a qualified specialist
• They cannot use internal medical reviewers alone to deny claims
• The opinion must be shared with the policyholder

💡 *Our Medical Agent validates medical necessity using ICD-10 codes and clinical guidelines automatically.*

cashless.response:
**Cashless vs Reimbursement Claims:**

**🏥 Cashless Claim:**
• Available only at **network hospitals**
• Insurer pays the hospital directly
• You only pay non-covered portions (co-pay, deductible, non-medical items)
• Authorization: 1 hour (planned), 2 hours (emergency) as per IRDAI
• **Advantage**: No upfront payment needed

**💵 Reimbursement Claim:**
• Available at **any hospital** (network or non-network)
• You pay the hospital first, then submit bills to insurer
• Insurer reimburses the covered amount
• Processing time: Up to 30 days after document submission
• **Advantage**: Freedom to choose any hospital

**Tips:**
• Always check if your hospital is in the insurer's network before admission
• Even for cashless, carry extra cash for non-covered items
• Keep ALL original bills — photocopies are not accepted for reimbursement
• In emergencies, you can go to any hospital and claim reimbursement later

policy_types.response:
**Types of Health Insurance Policies in India:**

**1. Individual Health Insurance** 👤
• Covers a single person
• Fixed sum insured for you alone
• Best for: Single individuals

**2. Family Floater** 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦
• Covers entire family under one sum insured
• Shared coverage — any family member can use it
• Cheaper than individual policies for each member
• Best for: Families with young members

**3. Group Health Insurance** 🏢
• Provided by employers
• Usually lower premiums
• Coverage may cease if you leave the company
• Often has lower sum insured — consider a top-up

**4. Top-Up/Super Top-Up** 📈
• Additional coverage above a deductible amount
• Very affordable way to increase coverage
• Super top-up aggregates bills across hospitalizations

**5. Critical Illness Policy** 🏥
• Lump sum payout on diagnosis of specific diseases
• Covers cancer, heart attack, stroke, organ transplant, etc.
• Payout irrespective of actual treatment cost

**6. Senior Citizen Health Insurance** 👴
• Designed for people above 60 years
• Higher premiums but essential coverage
• Some have no upper age limit for renewal

sum_insured.response:
**Sum Insured** is the maximum amount your insurer will pay in a policy year.

**How to Choose the Right Sum Insured:**
• Consider your city (metro cities need ₹10-25 lakhs minimum)
• Factor in age and family medical history
• Account for medical inflation (10-15% per year)
• Consider if you have employer coverage (and its limits)

**Recommended Sum Insured:**
• Tier 1 cities (Delhi, Mumbai, Bangalore): ₹15-25 lakhs
• Tier 2 cities: ₹10-15 lakhs
• With family history of serious illness: ₹20-50 lakhs

**Related Concepts:**
• **No Claim Bonus (NCB)**: Sum insured increases by 5-50% for every claim-free year
• **Restoration Benefit**: Sum insured gets restored (fully or partially) if used up
• **Super Top-Up**: Affordable way to increase effective coverage

claim_amount.response:
**Understanding Claim Amounts:**

The claim amount is the total medical expenses you're claiming from insurance. Here's what affects what you'll receive:

**Deductions That May Apply:**
• **Deductible**: Fixed amount deducted from claim (e.g., ₹10,000)
• **Co-pay**: Percentage you must bear (e.g., 20% of ₹5,00,000 = ₹1,00,000 your share)
• **Sub-limits**: Caps on specific items (room rent, ICU, surgeon fees)
• **Non-payable items**: Items not covered (toiletries, registration charges, etc.)

**Room Rent Sub-limit Example:**
If your policy allows room rent up to ₹5,000/day but you took a ₹8,000/day room:
• All bills may be proportionally reduced (5000/8000 = 62.5%)
• So a ₹5,00,000 bill may only get ₹3,12,500

💡 *Upload your medical bills and policy to our platform to get an accurate analysis of your eligible claim amount.*

portability.response:
**Health Insurance Portability:**

Portability allows you to switch your health insurance to another insurer while retaining:
• **Waiting period credits** already served
• **No Claim Bonus** accumulated

**How to Port:**
1. Apply to the new insurer at least **45 days** before renewal
2. New insurer requests data from old insurer
3. Old insurer must respond within **7 days**
4. New insurer decides within **15 days**

**IRDAI Rules:**
• Insurers **cannot** refuse portability without valid reason
• All waiting period credits must be transferred
• Coverage cannot be less than what was offered previously

💡 *Port during renewal to avoid any break in coverage.*

ncb.response:
**No Claim Bonus (NCB):**

NCB is a reward for not making claims. Types:

**1. Cumulative Bonus (Sum Insured Increase)**
• Sum insured increases by 5-50% for each claim-free year
• Can accumulate up to 100-200% of original sum insured
• If a claim is made, bonus may be reduced

**2. Premium Discount**
• Some insurers offer premium reduction instead
• Typically 5-10% per claim-free year

**IRDAI Rule:**
• NCB must be clearly mentioned in policy documents
• NCB credits must be portable when switching insurers
